head:
Chapter 4: AI-Based Driver Monitoring

This chapter transitions from design to practical implementation, detailing the AI models that form the heart of the driver monitoring system. The system's intel
tail: re, the data used for training, and a thorough evaluation of their performance. The findings for each system will be presented sequentially, providing a clear picture of their individual capabilities.

head:
4.1 Drowsiness Detection

4.1.1 ABSTRACT

Driver drowsiness is a significant factor in road accidents worldwide, often leading to severe injuries or fatalities. To address this, real-time driver monit
tail: ion. YOLO’s fast and accurate object detection capabilities allow it to identify critical facial landmarks associated with drowsiness in a single stage, ensuring timely detection with minimal latency.

head:
4.1.2

OVERVIEW

This project involves the development of a drowsiness detection system using computer vision. A pre-trained YOLOv11n model is employed for this task, which is fine-tuned to detect sta
tail: etection models, is known for its efficiency and precision, making it ideal for real-time applications in safety-critical environments such as driver monitoring systems or workplace fatigue detection.

head: YOLO (You Only Look Once) is one of the most popular object detection algorithms used in computer vision, known for its speed and efficiency. It is designed to detect and classify objects in images or

head:
4.1.4 LITERATURE REVIEW

Driver drowsiness detection has been a significant research focus due to its potential to reduce road accidents caused by fatigue. Various approaches have been explored, broad
tail: dly classified into physiological monitoring, vehiclebased metrics, and behavioral analysis. Physiological methods involve tracking signals like EEG, heart rate, and eye movement to detect drowsiness.

head: Although highly accurate, these methods are intrusive and impractical for real-world deployment. Vehicle-based metrics, such as lane deviation and steering angle analysis, provide indirect measures of
tail: il to capture early signs of fatigue. In contrast, behavioral analysis using computer vision techniques has gained prominence as a non-intrusive, effective solution for real-time drowsiness detection.

head: Early computer vision methods relied on handcrafted features and algorithms, such as Haar cascades and HOG with SVMs. While effective in controlled conditions, these approaches struggled with real-wor
tail:
ge regression problem.
Its real-time performance, scalability, and ability to predict multiple bounding boxes and class probabilities in one pass made it ideal for applications like driver monitoring.

head: Behavioral indicators of drowsiness, such as eyelid closure, yawning frequency, and head posture, have been extensively studied. These signs are critical for early detection of fatigue. The main idea
tail: ve been extensively studied. These signs are critical for early detection of fatigue. The main idea behind YOLO is to treat object detection as a regression problem. Instead of using a sliding window.

head: In YOLO, the architecture is designed to perform both object detection and classification efficiently in a single forward pass. A key difference between YOLO and traditional object detection models is

• Efficiency: Using 1
tail:
s layer. 1x1 convolutions drastically reduce the number of parameters while still enabling rich feature extraction and prediction.

• Spatial Hierarchy Preservation: In typical convolutional networks,

head:
• Spatial Hierarchy Preservation: In typical convolutional networks,

convolutional layers maintain spatial hierarchies (e.g., width and height). YOLO uses 1x1 convolutions to preserve this spatial hi
tail: the input image are maintained throughout the network. This allows the model to make predictions about bounding boxes and class probabilities at each pixel or grid cell, maintaining spatial coherence.

head:
Adaptive Drowsiness Detection System

This system employs an intelligent, adaptive approach to driver drowsiness detection. Instead of using a static alert threshold, it dynamically adjusts its sensit
tail:
l phases based on speed:

• Phase 1: Low-Speed Operation (Below 40 km/h)

o Eye Closure Threshold: Fixed at a maximum of 3.5 seconds.

o System Rationale: In low-risk environments like slow traffic or

head:
• Phase 1: Low-Speed Operation (Below 40 km/h)

o Eye Closure Threshold: Fixed at a maximum of 3.5 seconds.

o System Rationale: In low-risk environments like slow traffic or

parking, the system is a
tail:
o Eye Closure Threshold: Fixed at a maximum of 3.5 seconds.

o System Rationale: In low-risk environments like slow traffic or

parking, the system is at its most lenient to avoid unnecessary alerts.

head:
• Phase 2: Adaptive Operation (40 km/h to 140 km/h)

o Eye Closure Threshold: Decreases linearly from 3.5 seconds

down to 0.8 seconds.

o System Rationale: This is the core adaptive range. As speed
tail:
ationale: At high speeds where reaction times are

critical, the system operates at maximum sensitivity, providing the fastest possible warning for even brief lapses in attention.

Step 2: Clamp the ratio This ensures the ratio does not go below 0.0 or above 1.0, handling the flat parts of the graph. ratio=max(0.0,min(1.0,ratio))

head:
Step 2: Clamp the ratio This ensures the ratio does not go below 0.0 or above 1.0, handling the flat parts of the graph. ratio=max(0.0,min(1.0,ratio))

Step 3: Calculate the final threshold This inter
tail:
eshold This interpolates between the maximum and minimum thresholds based on the clamped ratio. threshold=thr_max − ratio×(thr_max − thr_min)

Where:

• speed_min = 40 (km/h)

• speed_max = 140 (km/h)

head:
• thr_max = 3.5 (s)

• thr_min = 0.8 (s)

Conclusion: The Primary Benefit

The key advantage of this design is Risk-Aware Safety. By correlating alert sensitivity with vehicle speed, the system provid
tail: t high speeds) while minimizing intrusive and potentially distracting false alarms in low-speed, low-risk scenarios. This creates a safer, smarter, and more user-friendly driver monitoring experience.

head:
Pre-trained YOLOv11n

The YOLOv11n model used in this project is pre-trained on the COCO dataset, which provides a robust foundation for object detection tasks. Pretraining on a large and diverse data
tail: et (Common Objects in Context) is one of the most widely used datasets in the computer vision community. It contains over 330,000 images annotated with detailed information about 80 object categories.

head: The dataset includes various object types that range from everyday items to animals and vehicles, making it versatile and suitable for a wide array of computer vision tasks, including object detection
tail: detect drowsiness, wakefulness, and yawning. This dataset consists of images captured in controlled environments where subjects either demonstrate alertness or display signs of drowsiness or yawning.

head:
Our custom dataset is classified into three categories:

1. Drowsiness: Images depicting individuals exhibiting signs of tiredness

or drowsiness.
tail:
Our custom dataset is classified into three categories:

1. Drowsiness: Images depicting individuals exhibiting signs of tiredness

or drowsiness.

head:
2. Awake: Images of individuals who are alert and awake.

3. Yawn: Images where individuals are caught in the act of yawning.

Transfer Learning Approach

In this project, we adopted a transfer learni
tail: ced training time and computational cost while maintaining high accuracy. It is especially beneficial when deploying on edge devices like the Raspberry Pi, where training from scratch is not feasible.

head:
The YOLOv11n model was fine-tuned on the custom dataset using the following Hyper-parameters:

• Batch Size: 128

• Epochs: 100

• Learning Rate: “auto” (Adapted during training based on performance)
tail:
1

Total number of freezed convolutional layers: 40

EarlyStopping: Training stopped early as no improvement observed in last 10 epochs. Best results observed at epoch 52, best model saved as best.pt.

head:
4.2 Hand Gesture Recognition

4.2.1 ABSTRACT

This task is a real-time driver gesture recognition system built with MediaPipe, OpenCV, and Python. It recognizes hand gestures through a webcam and visu
tail: esture results along with the landmarks of the detected hands. You can use this task to recognize specific hand gestures from a user, and invoke application features that correspond to those gestures.

head: This task operates on image data with a machine learning (ML) model, and accepts either static data or a continuous stream. The task outputs hand landmarks in image coordinates, hand landmarks in worl
tail: data or a continuous stream. The task outputs hand landmarks in image coordinates, hand landmarks in world coordinates, handedness (left/right hand), and the hand gesture categories of multiple hands.

head:
Figure 4.10: Gesture Recognition example

This section describes the capabilities, inputs, outputs, and configuration options of this task.

Features

• Input Image Processing

The Gesture Recognizer accepts one of the following data types as input:

• Still images

• Decoded video frames

Task Outputs

The Gesture Recognizer provides the following outputs:

• Categories of hand gestures

• Handedness of detected hands

• Landmarks of detected hands in image coordinat
tail:
ndedness of detected hands

• Landmarks of detected hands in image coordinates

• Landmarks of detected hands in world coordinates

4.2.4 Models

The Gesture Recognizer uses a model bundle with two pre-packaged model bundles: a hand landmark model bundle and a gesture classification model bundle.
tail:
on hand geometry.

Hand landmark model bundle

Figure 4.11 illustrates the hand landmark model bundle detects the keypoint localization of 21 hand-knuckle coordinates within the detected hand regions.

head:
The model was trained on approximately 30K real-world images, as well as several rendered synthetic hand models imposed over various backgrounds.
See the definition of the 21 landmarks below:

Figure
tail: Palm detection model localizes the region of hands from the whole input image, and the hand landmarks detection model finds the landmarks on the cropped hand image defined by the palm detection model.

head: Since palm detection model is much more time consuming, in Video mode or Live stream mode, Gesture Recognizer uses bounding box defined by the detected hand landmarks in the current frame to localize
tail:
ze these common hand gestures:

Unrecognized gesture → label: Unknown

Closed fist → label: Closed_Fist

Open palm → label: Open_Palm

Pointing up → label: Pointing_Up

Thumbs down → label: Thumb_Down

If the model detects hands but does not recognize a gesture, the gesture recognizer returns a result of "None". If the model does not detect hands, th
tail:
nds, the gesture recognizer returns empty.

The gesture classification model bundle contains two step neural network pipeline with a gesture embedding model followed by a gesture classification model.

head:
The gesture classification model bundle contains two step neural network pipeline with a gesture embedding model followed by a gesture classification model.

The gesture embedding model encodes the im
tail: ns the canned gestures classifier, which detects the 7 common hand gestures introduced above. You can extend the model bundle to recognize more gestures by training your own custom gesture classifier.
